---
title: "Evaluating a New Pandemic Influenza Outbreak in Kyoto"
subtitle: "A Simulation Exercise — Summer Bootcamp of Infectious Disease Modeling, Kyoto 2024"
author: "Andy Chu (Group 2)"
date: "2024-08-10"
---

This was a group project from the Summer Bootcamp of Infectious Disease
Modeling (Kyoto, 2024): a week-long intensive course where small groups
built and presented a simulation exercise from scratch. Group 2 — Zhen
Zhi Chu (Andy), Jongmin Lee, Thein Min Swe, Sachiko Ichihara, Yuri
Amemiya, and Keita Shimizu — modeled a hypothetical new pandemic
influenza outbreak reaching Kyoto, and compared public health
interventions.

[Download the full slide deck (PDF)](Summer_camp_slides.pdf)

## Introduction

Pandemic influenza differs from seasonal influenza in a key way: with no
pre-existing immunity in the population, historical pandemics (Spanish
flu 1918–20, Asian flu 1957–58, Hong Kong flu 1968–69, Russian flu
1977–79, and H1N1/09) have caused far larger and less predictable waves
than any typical winter season. H1N1/09 itself combined swine, human, and
avian gene segments and spread worldwide between April 2009 and August
2010, with roughly 491,000 lab-confirmed cases and 18,000 deaths
reported by WHO. In Japan, the first confirmed cases (high school
students with no travel history) triggered a rapid escalation to
nationwide school closures within days.

The exercise imagined a new, unknown influenza-like illness with
epidemiological features similar to 2009 pandemic influenza, already
five weeks into an outbreak in Kobe and Osaka, with one infected child
returning to Kyoto. The group's task was to evaluate outbreak
preparedness and response options — school closure, mask-wearing, and
vaccination — before the disease could take hold in Kyoto.

## Methods

The group built a deterministic, age-structured (adult / child)
compartmental model stratified further by vaccination status, with
ordinary differential equations governing transitions between
susceptible, infected, recovered, and dead states. Parameters were
grounded in Kyoto's 2009 census (adult population 2,275,000; child
population 347,000) and literature estimates: a basic reproduction
number R₀ = 1.482 (age-dependent next-generation matrix), a mean
generation time of 4 days, mask-wearing impact from coverage × efficacy
(0.5 × 0.3), and one-dose vaccine effectiveness against susceptibility,
infectiousness, and death (α₁ = 0.6, α₂ = 0.5, α₃ = 0.18). Six scenarios
were simulated over a one-year period: no intervention; school closure
alone (3 weeks); mask-wearing alone (all year); and three
mask-plus-vaccination combinations differing in mask duration and how
early vaccination began.

## Results

With no intervention, the epidemic peaked around day 98 with a final
size of about 301,000 infections (11.5% of the 2.6 million population)
and 11 deaths. School closure alone barely changed the outcome — it
delayed the peak by about three weeks but left the final size and death
toll essentially unchanged, since the intervention window was too short
relative to the epidemic's overall duration. Mask-wearing alone was far
more effective on its own, cutting the final size to about 7.3% and
deaths to 6, by delaying and lowering the peak throughout the year.

Combining mask-wearing with vaccination performed best overall,
especially when vaccination started earlier: starting mask-wearing from
day 0 and vaccination from day 150 (rather than day 180) produced the
smallest final size (6.6%) and fewest deaths (5) of all six scenarios —
about a 43% reduction in final size and 55% reduction in deaths relative
to doing nothing.

## Discussion & Recommendations

School closure is useful for containing localized clusters but is
difficult to sustain for long periods, limiting its stand-alone impact
across a full epidemic wave. Mask-wearing (as a public health and social
measure) can meaningfully lower and delay the epidemic peak and ease the
burden on hospitals, though even 100% coverage cannot push R_t below 1
on its own. Vaccination directly removes people from the susceptible
pool and has both direct and indirect population-level effects, but
timing matters — earlier vaccination consistently outperformed later
vaccination in the simulations. The group's overall recommendation was a
three-pronged, coordinated approach: timely school closure around the
epidemic peak, mask-wearing from day 0 sustained throughout the
outbreak, and vaccination rolled out as early as possible — since
combined interventions consistently outperformed any single measure
alone.

**Limitations:** the exercise modeled a hypothetical, not a real,
pathogen; the main analysis used simplified, constant intervention
parameters without sensitivity analysis or confidence intervals; and it
did not model human behavior change (e.g., spontaneous self-isolation).
An appendix extension explored a stochastic version of the model
(Gillespie algorithm) to characterize the probability of outbreak
extinction versus sustained epidemic spread under different importation
and intervention-timing assumptions.
